name: Delphi
description: Delphi, nestled on the slopes of Mount Parnassus in Greece, was revered as the center of the world in ancient Greek religion and mythology. This sacred precinct, dedicated to Apollo, housed the Oracle of Delphi, the Pythia, who delivered prophecies that influenced political decisions, military campaigns, and personal matters across the ancient Mediterranean world. The site's history stretches back to at least the Mycenaean period, with evidence suggesting earlier chthonic deities were worshipped there before Apollo's ascendancy. The sanctuary included the Temple of Apollo, the Athenian Treasury, a theater, a stadium, and numerous monuments and statues dedicated by various city-states seeking favor with the god and his prophetic abilities. The Delphic Games, second only to the Olympics in prestige, were held every four years, further cementing Delphi's importance as a panhellenic center. It was also believed to be the site where the omphalos, the navel of the world, was located, marking the point where Zeus released two eagles from opposite ends of the earth, and they met at Delphi.
subtitle: Ancient Greek sanctuary and home of the Oracle of Delphi.
significance: Delphi was considered the center of the world and the most important religious sanctuary in ancient Greece, known for its oracle and influence on political and personal decisions.

keyMyths.title: The Founding of the Oracle
keyMyths.description: According to legend, Apollo slew the serpent Python, who guarded the site, and established his oracle there. Python was said to be a child of Gaia, representing the earth's primordial power. Apollo's victory symbolized the triumph of order and reason over chaos, establishing Delphi as a center of divine wisdom and prophecy. The Pythian Games were held in commemoration of this event.

keyMyths.title: Oedipus and the Oracle
keyMyths.description: Oedipus consulted the Oracle of Delphi to learn about his destiny. The Oracle prophesied that he would kill his father and marry his mother, a fate he desperately tried to avoid. Ironically, his attempts to escape the prophecy only led him to fulfill it, highlighting the power and inevitability of the Oracle's pronouncements and the tragic nature of fate in Greek mythology.

keyMyths.title: Heracles and the Tripod
keyMyths.description: When Heracles sought guidance from the Oracle about atonement for killing his family, she initially refused to answer. In a fit of rage, Heracles attempted to steal the sacred tripod, the seat of the Pythia, leading to a struggle with Apollo. Zeus intervened to prevent further conflict, demonstrating the importance of respecting the divine authority of Delphi, even for heroes.

keyMyths.title: The Sibyl of Delphi
keyMyths.description: Before Apollo's arrival, some accounts say Delphi was the location of the Sibyl, a prophetic woman. She was said to have sung her oracles from a rock. This suggests a pre-existing tradition of prophecy at the site, later incorporated into the worship of Apollo. The legend highlights the evolution of religious beliefs at Delphi.

sources.author: Parke, H.W.
sources.description: A comprehensive study of Greek oracles, including Delphi, examining their history, function, and influence.
sources.text: A comprehensive study of Greek oracles, including Delphi, examining their history, function, and influence.

sources.author: Fontenrose, Joseph
sources.work: Python: A Study of Delphic Myth and Its Origins
sources.description: An in-depth analysis of the myth of Python and its significance to the establishment of the Delphic Oracle.
sources.text: An in-depth analysis of the myth of Python and its significance to the establishment of the Delphic Oracle.

sources.author: Morgan, Catherine
sources.work: Early Greek States Beyond the Polis
sources.description: Explores the role of Delphi and other panhellenic sanctuaries in the formation of early Greek state identities.
